steps.title: Child protection
steps.text: When is a child eligible to receive PEP (Post-Exposure Prophylaxis) following suspected sexual abuse? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: PEP should be given if the child is HIV-negative and presents within 72 hours, regardless of the assailant’s test result, due to the possibility of window-period transmission. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: When the child is HIV-negative and presents within 72 hours, regardless of the assailant’s HIV status
When the child presents more than 72 hours after the incident and the assailant is HIV-positive
When the child is HIV-negative and the assailant has a known positive HIV status
When the child is under 10 years old and the caregiver agrees to HIV testing

steps.title: Child protection
steps.text: What is the correct timing for giving PEP in a child suspected of sexual abuse? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The guideline is clear: do not delay. Test and treat during the same clinical encounter. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: After HIV test results are reviewed by a consultant
Only after police consent is obtained
Immediately—HIV test and PEP should be given at the same visit
Within 7 days of the incident

steps.title: Child protection
steps.text: Which of the following is the correct approach to documenting a medical report for suspected abuse? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Documentation may serve as evidence. The report must be factual, legible, and include verbatim quotes and clear injury descriptions. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Use only your own interpretation of the child’s statements
Avoid sketches and focus on brief text notes
Write legibly, summarize physical findings in detail, and use direct quotations
Only record the final diagnosis and treatment given

steps.title: Child protection
steps.text: Which of the following is part of the antibiotic regimen to prevent or treat STIs in a child after sexual abuse? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: The guideline outlines a multi-drug regimen to cover a range of pathogens. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Only oral amoxicillin for 5 days
Metronidazole, Erythromycin/Azithromycin, Gentamicin, and Benzathine Penicillin
Ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline
Azithromycin as a single drug therapy

steps.title: Child protection
steps.text: What is an essential action to take alongside medical treatment for suspected abuse? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Early involvement of social and legal authorities is a critical part of the response. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Discharge the child immediately after stabilizing
Keep the case confidential and avoid involving authorities
Inform and involve social workers and police as early as possible
Wait until follow-up to notify any non-medical services

steps.title: Child protection
steps.text: According to the guideline, which antiretroviral regimens are recommended for PEP in children following suspected sexual abuse? [cite: 1]
steps.rationale: Children should receive ABC/3TC + DTG or TDF/3TC/DTG depending on weight (TDF combo is typically for children over 30 kg). The regimen must start immediately and continue for 30 days. [cite: 1]

textChoices.text: Zidovudine and Nevirapine
ABC/3TC + DTG or TDF/3TC/DTG based on the child's body weight
Tenofovir only, regardless of weight
DTG monotherapy to reduce pill burden
